headline.title: Headline inflation
headline.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my

core.title: Core inflation
core.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my

unemployment.title: Unemployment rate
unemployment.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my

cpiDecomposition.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia CPI publication
cpiDecomposition.method: Official division weight multiplied by the division year-on-year inflation rate
cpiDecomposition.warning: This is a transparent weighted-pressure estimate. Malaysia uses a chained CPI, so division estimates need not add exactly to headline inflation; the reconciliation gap is shown rather than hidden.

forecast.methodLabel: Pseudo-real-time backtest using conservative release lags

scenario.warning: A sensitivity overlay based on historical ARIMAX associations. It is not the selected forecast unless ARIMAX wins the backtest, and it does not identify causal effects.

methodology.model: Level on intercept, linear trend, and one-month lag
methodology.confirmation: Classical Chow test with within-indicator Holm correction
methodology.robustness: HAC/Newey-West joint Wald test and full-sample CUSUM

headline.narrative: BIC selected a stable single-regime specification; no structural break candidate is reported. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
core.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2021-11. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.35 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
unemployment.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2022-04. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.33 units lower; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
opr.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2022-05. The adjacent-regime mean was 1.05 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

fx.narrative: The latest supported parameter shift is estimated near 2014-09. The adjacent-regime mean was 0.70 units higher; both the Holm-adjusted Chow and HAC tests are below 5%. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

warnings: A 12-month minimum regime was required because the available history is short; treat break dates as lower-confidence.
The level series does not reject a unit root at 5%; level and trend breaks may partly reflect persistence.
mgs.narrative: BIC selected a stable single-regime specification; no structural break candidate is reported. This is evidence of parameter instability, not proof that a nearby event caused the change.

narratives.performance: The FBM KLCI gained 10.3% over the latest year, with 9.4% annualised volatility and a 6.5% maximum drawdown during that window.
narratives.macro: The index can respond to earnings, global risk appetite, commodity prices, interest rates and the ringgit. These co-movements are context, not evidence that any one macro variable caused the market move.

economicStructure.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my
economicStructure.frequency: Annual totals aggregated from quarterly observations
economicStructure.measure: GDP at current prices by production sector

years.narrative: Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2015, accounting for 54.7% of nominal GDP.

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2016, accounting for 55.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +10.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +46.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2017, accounting for 54.5% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +16.7%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +57.4 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2018, accounting for 55.6% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +11.1%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +57.6 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2019, accounting for 56.9% of nominal GDP. Services recorded the fastest current-price increase at +6.8%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +55.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2020, accounting for 57.6% of nominal GDP. Agriculture recorded the fastest current-price increase at +5.7%, while Agriculture added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +6.3 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2021, accounting for 54.4% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +29.3%, while Manufacturing added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +46.5 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2022, accounting for 53.4% of nominal GDP. Mining and quarrying recorded the fastest current-price increase at +41.7%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +117.6 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2023, accounting for 56.0% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +8.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +63.8 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2024, accounting for 56.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +18.5%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +64.1 billion).

Services was Malaysia's largest production sector in 2025, accounting for 57.2% of nominal GDP. Construction recorded the fastest current-price increase at +11.3%, while Services added the largest ringgit amount to the annual change (RM +72.1 billion).

economicStructure.note: Sector shares describe where value added is produced. They are not government revenue, company profit or household income. Current-price changes combine real output and price effects.
economicStructure.message: Official quarterly observations validated; only complete calendar years are published

demand.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my
demand.frequency: Annual totals aggregated from quarterly observations
demand.measure: GDP at current prices by expenditure type

years.narrative: The expenditure view for 2015 looks consumption-led. Private consumption made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2016 looks consumption-led. Private consumption made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2017 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2018 looks consumption-led. Private consumption made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2019 looks consumption-led. Private consumption made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2020 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2021 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2022 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2023 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2024 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.
The expenditure view for 2025 looks export-led. Exports of goods and services made the largest absolute contribution to the annual GDP change among the main demand components.

demand.note: Imports are shown as a positive ringgit flow but subtract from GDP in the expenditure identity. Inventories are volatile and should not be read as a stable demand engine.
demand.message: Official quarterly expenditure observations validated; complete years only

growthDrivers.summary: Production-side GDP is still anchored by Services. The latest expenditure-side screen points to Exports of goods and services as the main annual demand-side driver where data are available.
growthDrivers.message: Production and expenditure GDP views validated

externalSector.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my

narratives.performance: In the latest month, Malaysia recorded RM 182.7 billion of goods exports and RM 154.0 billion of goods imports, leaving a RM 28.8 billion trade balance.
narratives.macro: Goods trade matters for the ringgit, manufacturing demand and imported-cost pressure, but it excludes services trade and does not by itself explain GDP or market performance.

externalSector.message: Official monthly trade headline data validated

balancePayments.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my

narratives.externalPosition: Malaysia's latest current account is a RM 15.2 billion surplus. The financial account balance is RM +27.4 billion and the official reserve account balance is RM -16.4 billion.
narratives.ringgitContext: The balance of payments helps explain external funding pressure, but exchange rates also respond to interest-rate expectations, risk appetite and global US dollar conditions.

balancePayments.message: Official quarterly BOP component balances validated

householdPressure.summary: Household pressure is low, led by debt service in the current rule-based screen.

components.label: Cost of living
components.evidence: Headline inflation is 1.9% and core inflation is 1.9%.
components.watch: Track your own food, transport, rent and utilities basket rather than relying only on national CPI.

components.label: Debt service
components.evidence: The OPR is 2.75%, which anchors many floating-rate loan discussions.
components.watch: Stress-test mortgage, hire-purchase and personal-loan instalments before taking new commitments.

components.label: Job and income
components.watch: National unemployment can hide weaker hiring in specific sectors or regions.

components.label: Imported spending
components.watch: Foreign-currency subscriptions, travel, imported goods and overseas education can move differently from local CPI.

components.label: Market wealth
components.watch: Equity performance is not a savings plan; align risk with time horizon and cash needs.

scenarios.title: Variable-rate borrower
scenarios.prompt: If policy-rate pressure remains elevated, check whether repayments still fit after a 50-100 bp stress test.
scenarios.limit: Actual loan pricing depends on lender, tenure, collateral and borrower profile.

scenarios.title: New property buyer
scenarios.prompt: Compare rent, ownership costs, maintenance and cash buffer after down payment before relying on future price appreciation.
scenarios.limit: This is not a property recommendation and does not assess any location.

scenarios.title: Early-career worker
scenarios.prompt: Use stable labour periods to build emergency savings, portfolio evidence and transferable skills.
scenarios.limit: Occupation-specific hiring can diverge from national unemployment.

scenarios.title: Long-term investor
scenarios.prompt: Use diversification and time horizon rather than the latest Bursa move as the decision anchor.
scenarios.limit: Returns exclude dividends, fees and taxes and are not forecasts.

householdPressure.disclaimer: Educational household-pressure screen only. It is not personalised financial, investment, property, tax, legal or career advice.

sectorDeepDive.summary: The sector deep-dive maps production GDP shares to export exposure, Bursa context and recent current-price growth for 2025.

sectors.name: Services
sectors.narrative: Services accounts for 57.2% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is +6.6% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectors.name: Manufacturing
sectors.narrative: Manufacturing accounts for 22.1% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is +2.8% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectors.name: Agriculture
sectors.narrative: Agriculture accounts for 8.2% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is +5.9% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectors.name: Mining and quarrying
sectors.narrative: Mining and quarrying accounts for 6.8% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is -9.2% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectors.name: Construction
sectors.narrative: Construction accounts for 4.3% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is +11.3% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectors.name: Import duties
sectors.narrative: Import duties accounts for 1.3% of nominal GDP in 2025. Its current-price output change is +16.2% where prior-year comparison is available.

sectorDeepDive.source: Department of Statistics Malaysia via data.gov.my
sectorDeepDive.limitations: Sector screens describe broad economic structure. They do not forecast company earnings or identify investable stocks.

macroTimeline.summary: The timeline combines official Malaysian events, data-selected structural breaks and the latest market observation. Event proximity is context, not causal proof.
macroTimeline.limitations: Structural-break candidates are exploratory and may change after official data revisions.

riskHeatmap.summary: The current macro risk screen is low. The highest-pressure signals are Policy rate, 10-year MGS yield, Bursa large-cap market.
riskHeatmap.method: Deterministic rules combine latest levels, recent changes, historical percentile checks and market/trade summaries. Scores are descriptive screens, not forecasts or causal estimates.

items.label: Headline inflation
items.rule: Low below 2%, moderate from 2-3%, high at 3% or above.
items.watch: Watch food, transport and administered-price categories.

items.label: Core inflation
items.evidence: Latest core inflation is 1.9%; 3-month change is -0.2 pp.
items.rule: Low below 2%, moderate from 2-3%, high at 3% or above.
items.watch: Persistent core pressure matters more than one monthly headline move.

items.label: Labour market
items.rule: Low below 4%, moderate from 4-5%, high at 5% or above.
items.watch: National unemployment can hide sector and regional weakness.

items.label: Policy rate
items.rule: Low below 2.50%, moderate from 2.50-3.25%, high above 3.25%.
items.watch: The real policy stance also depends on expected inflation.

items.label: USD/MYR pressure
items.evidence: USD/MYR is RM 4.0375, at the 65.8th percentile of this dashboard history.
items.rule: Higher USD/MYR levels receive higher imported-cost pressure scores.
items.watch: A weaker ringgit can help exporters while raising imported costs.

items.label: 10-year MGS yield
items.rule: Low below 3.50%, moderate from 3.50-4.25%, high above 4.25%.
items.watch: Bond yields reflect policy expectations, term premium and global rates.

items.label: Bursa large-cap market
items.evidence: KLCI one-year price return is +10.3% with a 6.5% max drawdown.
items.rule: Higher pressure when one-year return is negative or drawdown is large.
items.watch: Index performance excludes dividends, fees and taxes.

items.label: GDP growth drivers
items.rule: Lower pressure when a clear demand engine is visible; higher when only partial evidence is available.
items.watch: Current-price GDP combines volume and price effects.

items.label: Goods trade balance
items.evidence: Latest goods trade balance is RM +28.8 billion; exports YoY +36.9%, imports YoY +20.0%.
items.rule: Higher pressure when trade balance is negative or imports grow faster than exports.
items.watch: Goods trade excludes services and income flows.

latestBrief.headline: Malaysia's latest macro reading is low pressure, with headline inflation at 1.9% and core inflation at 1.9%.
whatChanged: Headline inflation moved -0.1 percentage points from the prior monthly release.
The ringgit moved -0.02 against the US dollar over the latest three-month dashboard window.
The FBM KLCI one-year price return is +10.3%, while the latest goods trade balance is RM +28.8 billion.
whyItMayHaveHappened: Inflation changes can reflect category-level CPI pressure, policy effects, administered prices and imported costs.
Exchange-rate and bond-yield changes may reflect both Malaysian conditions and global interest-rate or risk-appetite shifts.
GDP and trade readings help separate domestic demand from external demand, but they are not causal proof for market moves.
watchNext: Policy rate: The real policy stance also depends on expected inflation.
10-year MGS yield: Bond yields reflect policy expectations, term premium and global rates.
Bursa large-cap market: Index performance excludes dividends, fees and taxes.
implications: Individuals should stress-test savings, debt repayments and job resilience against the highest-pressure signals.
Companies should review pricing, cash flow, FX exposure and hiring plans using their own contracts and margins.
The three-month inflation forecast ends at 1.80%, but the prediction intervals are more important than the point estimate.
latestBrief.disclaimer: Educational macroeconomic briefing only. It is not personalised financial, investment, property, legal, tax or career advice.

decisionGuide.title: Decision guide for the current Malaysian economy
decisionGuide.summary: Malaysia currently combines 1.9% headline inflation, a 2.75% OPR, 3.0% unemployment and a +10.3% one-year KLCI price return. The useful response is disciplined scenario planning—not a single buy, sell or career instruction.

signals.label: Unemployment
signals.reading: the national unemployment rate is relatively low

signals.label: USD/MYR
signals.reading: ringgit cost of one US dollar

individuals.title: Build liquidity before taking more market risk
individuals.evidence: Headline inflation is 1.9% and core inflation is 1.9%, so cash purchasing power still changes even when inflation is moderate.
actions: Estimate essential monthly commitments and work toward an emergency buffer suited to income stability.
Keep emergency money accessible; compare deposit terms, withdrawal limits and PIDM protection before choosing an account.
Automate a realistic monthly transfer instead of relying on leftover cash.
individuals.watch: Job stability, household-specific expenses and any variable-rate debt matter more than the national average alone.

individuals.title: Use goals and diversification, not the latest index move
individuals.evidence: The FBM KLCI's latest one-year price return is +10.3% and its measured one-year volatility is 9.4%.
actions: Match any equity allocation to the time horizon, loss capacity and need for near-term cash.
Review diversification across companies, sectors and asset types; the KLCI represents large-cap shares, not the whole market.
Verify that intermediaries and products are authorised by the Securities Commission before transferring money.
individuals.watch: Recent performance is not a forecast. Returns shown here exclude dividends, fees and taxes.

individuals.title: Test affordability before relying on property appreciation
individuals.evidence: The OPR is 2.75% and the 10-year MGS yield is 3.66%, both relevant reference points for financing conditions.
actions: Compare the full ownership cost with renting, including maintenance, assessment, insurance, vacancy and transaction costs.
Recalculate repayments under a higher-rate scenario and a temporary income interruption.
Preserve a separate cash reserve after the deposit and purchase costs.
individuals.watch: Property suitability depends on location, financing terms, holding period and personal cash flow—not national inflation alone.

individuals.title: Use the stable labour picture to improve resilience
individuals.evidence: National unemployment is 3.0%; the national unemployment rate is relatively low, but this does not describe every occupation or region.
actions: Track your own essential-cost inflation rather than assuming the headline CPI matches your household basket.
Use stable employment periods to build portable skills, update professional evidence and explore market salary ranges.
Direct pay increases or bonuses toward high-cost debt, emergency savings and long-term goals before lifestyle expansion.
individuals.watch: Sector hiring, contract type and individual employability can diverge sharply from the national unemployment rate.

individuals.title: Review variable-rate and short-tenor commitments
individuals.evidence: The dashboard heatmap labels policy-rate pressure as moderate and 10-year MGS pressure as moderate.
actions: List every debt repayment date, rate type and reset date.
Check whether a higher instalment still leaves room for essentials and emergency savings.
Avoid using short-term promotional rates as the only affordability test.
individuals.watch: Lending rates depend on individual credit profile, bank policy and product terms, not only national benchmark rates.

individuals.title: Watch imported-cost pressure in your own spending
individuals.evidence: USD/MYR is RM 4.0375; latest goods imports are RM 154.0 billion where trade data are available.
actions: Track recurring imported or foreign-currency-linked spending separately.
Compare total cost after shipping, tax, warranties and exchange-rate conversion.
Keep subscription and discretionary spending flexible when currency pressure is elevated.
individuals.watch: A national exchange-rate move does not affect every household basket equally.

companies.title: Review debt sensitivity and idle-cash policy
companies.evidence: The OPR is 2.75% while the 10-year MGS yield is 3.66%. Borrowing still carries a meaningful financing cost.
actions: Run base, higher-rate and revenue-shock cash-flow cases before refinancing or expanding debt.
Match cash maturities to payroll, tax and supplier obligations rather than maximising yield alone.
Separate committed facilities from genuinely available liquidity and monitor covenant headroom.
companies.watch: Actual bank pricing depends on credit risk, collateral, tenor and facility structure.

companies.title: Respond to cost pressure at product level
companies.evidence: Headline inflation is 1.9% and core inflation is 1.9% (positive but moderate); category pressures remain uneven.
actions: Track input, wage, freight and energy costs separately rather than applying a blanket CPI uplift.
Review gross margin by product and customer before changing prices or promotions.
Use smaller, explainable adjustments where demand is price-sensitive.
companies.watch: CPI measures consumer prices and is not a direct index of any company's input-cost structure.

companies.title: Manage net currency exposure, not exchange-rate headlines
companies.evidence: The latest monthly USD/MYR observation is RM 4.0375 per US dollar.
actions: Map contracted foreign-currency receipts and payments by date, currency and certainty.
Use natural offsets first and discuss appropriate hedging instruments with regulated banking providers.
Test quotations and margins under adverse exchange-rate scenarios.
companies.watch: The dashboard describes USD/MYR movements; it does not forecast a profitable hedge or currency direction.

companies.title: Link hiring and capital spending to demand evidence
companies.evidence: Unemployment is 3.0% and the KLCI's latest one-year price performance is positive at +10.3%.
actions: Prioritise roles tied to bottlenecks, revenue quality or measurable productivity gains.
Stage capital projects with decision gates instead of treating broad market optimism as demand proof.
Model downside demand, financing and FX assumptions before approving irreversible expenditure.
companies.watch: A stock index and national unemployment rate are broad signals, not company-specific revenue forecasts.

companies.title: Tie inventory decisions to FX and trade evidence
companies.evidence: Goods imports grew +20.0% year on year, while USD/MYR is RM 4.0375.
actions: Separate essential stock buffers from speculative over-ordering.
Reprice landed cost assumptions using adverse FX and freight scenarios.
Review supplier currency, payment timing and contract pass-through clauses.
companies.watch: Trade aggregates do not reveal firm-level demand, supplier reliability or margin quality.

companies.title: Set decision gates around the highest-risk signals
companies.evidence: The current heatmap is low pressure, led by Headline inflation, Core inflation.
actions: Define measurable triggers before hiring, capex, refinancing or price changes.
Assign owners for inflation, FX, cash-flow and sales indicators.
Review decisions monthly after official releases instead of reacting to headlines.
companies.watch: A heatmap is a monitoring tool; it cannot replace customer, supplier and balance-sheet evidence.

decisionGuide.disclaimer: General educational scenarios only. They do not consider income, liabilities, tax, risk tolerance, business contracts or objectives. They are not personalised financial, investment, property, legal, tax or career advice.

dataOperations.vintagePolicy: A new immutable CPI snapshot is stored whenever the published CPI period changes.

narratives.snapshot: Headline inflation eased to 1.9% in the latest release, while core inflation was 1.9%.
narratives.forecast: The selected ARIMAX model places the three-month central forecast at 1.80%. Prediction intervals, rather than the point estimate alone, should guide interpretation.
narratives.financial: Inflation, policy rates, the ringgit and bond yields move together through several channels, but these descriptive relationships are not causal estimates or trading recommendations.

sources.message: Official quarterly observations validated; only complete calendar years are published
Official monthly trade headline data validated
Official quarterly BOP component balances validated

monthlyReport.title: MacroLens Malaysia monthly research brief

sections.heading: Main macro reading
sections.body: Malaysia's latest macro reading is low pressure, with headline inflation at 1.9% and core inflation at 1.9%.

sections.heading: Risk heatmap
sections.body: The current macro risk screen is low. The highest-pressure signals are Policy rate, 10-year MGS yield, Bursa large-cap market.

sections.heading: Household pressure
sections.body: Household pressure is low, led by debt service in the current rule-based screen.

sections.heading: External position
sections.body: Malaysia's latest current account is a RM 15.2 billion surplus. The financial account balance is RM +27.4 billion and the official reserve account balance is RM -16.4 billion.

sections.heading: Forecast
sections.body: The selected ARIMAX model places the three-month central forecast at 1.80%. Prediction intervals, rather than the point estimate alone, should guide interpretation.

monthlyReport.disclaimer: This report is generated from validated dashboard data and is educational analysis, not personalised financial advice.
